event: The earliest known human settlers, archaic hunter-gatherers, reach the island of Hispaniola from the Central American or northern South American mainland.
Ceramic-making Saladoid peoples migrating from the Orinoco region of South America begin settling Hispaniola, introducing agriculture and pottery.
The Taino culture emerges on Hispaniola, developing chiefdoms (cacicazgos), cassava farming, and the ceremonial ball game.
Taino society reaches its height, organizing the island into several large chiefdoms ruled by caciques.
Christopher Columbus lands on Hispaniola, claims it for Spain, and founds the settlement of La Navidad with wreckage from the Santa Maria.
Columbus returns to find La Navidad destroyed and founds La Isabela, the first intended permanent European town in the Americas.
Bartholomew Columbus founds Santo Domingo, which becomes the oldest continuously inhabited European-established city in the Americas.
Spanish forces under Governor Nicolas de Ovando massacre Taino at Xaragua, accelerating the conquest and enslavement of the indigenous population.
Friar Antonio de Montesinos delivers a famous sermon in Santo Domingo denouncing Spanish abuse of the Taino, sparking debate over indigenous rights.
The cacique Enriquillo leads a thirteen-year Taino rebellion in the Bahoruco mountains against Spanish rule.
The Universidad Autonoma de Santo Domingo is established, becoming the oldest university in the Americas.
English privateer Francis Drake captures and sacks Santo Domingo, holding the city for ransom.
Spain forcibly depopulates the western and northern coasts (the Devastaciones de Osorio) to curb smuggling, leaving them open to foreign settlers.
The Treaty of Ryswick formally cedes the western third of Hispaniola to France, creating the colony of Saint-Domingue alongside Spanish Santo Domingo.
By the Treaty of Basel, Spain cedes the entire colony of Santo Domingo to France.
Haitian leader Toussaint Louverture occupies Santo Domingo and abolishes slavery across the whole island.
Spanish-colonial forces, with British naval help, retake Santo Domingo, beginning the period known as 'Espana Boba.'
Jose Nunez de Caceres proclaims the short-lived independent state of Spanish Haiti, also called the Ephemeral Independence.
Haitian President Jean-Pierre Boyer invades and annexes the eastern part of the island, beginning a 22-year Haitian occupation.
Juan Pablo Duarte founds the secret society La Trinitaria to organize resistance against Haitian rule and achieve independence.
The Dominican Republic declares independence from Haiti on February 27, founding the nation as a sovereign state.
Pedro Santana defeats a major Haitian invasion at the Battle of Las Carreras, consolidating Dominican independence.
President Pedro Santana returns the country to Spanish colonial rule, ending its first period of independence.
The War of Restoration breaks out as Dominicans rise against renewed Spanish rule.
Spain withdraws and the Dominican Republic restores its independence following the War of Restoration.
Ulises Heureaux begins his dominant role in Dominican politics, ruling as a dictator until his assassination in 1899.
The United States takes control of Dominican customs receipts to manage the country's foreign debt under a financial receivership.
United States Marines occupy the Dominican Republic, beginning an eight-year military occupation.
The United States ends its military occupation and Dominican civilian rule is restored under President Horacio Vasquez.
Rafael Leonidas Trujillo seizes power, beginning a brutal 31-year dictatorship.
Trujillo orders the Parsley Massacre, in which the army kills thousands of Haitians living near the border.
The Dominican Republic becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Trujillo's agents assassinate the Mirabal sisters, opponents of the regime, later commemorated by the International Day for the Elimination of Violence against Women.
Rafael Trujillo is assassinated, ending his long dictatorship and opening a turbulent transition.
Juan Bosch wins the country's first free elections in decades, becoming president.
A military coup overthrows the elected government of Juan Bosch after only seven months in office.
Civil war erupts between Constitutionalists and the military, prompting a United States-led intervention of tens of thousands of troops.
Joaquin Balaguer is elected president, beginning a long and recurring dominance of Dominican politics.
Antonio Guzman of the PRD wins the presidency, marking a peaceful democratic transfer of power from Balaguer.
Leonel Fernandez is elected president, ending Balaguer's era and launching market-oriented economic reforms.
Hurricane Georges devastates the country, killing hundreds and causing extensive damage.
A major banking crisis triggered by the collapse of Baninter sends the economy into recession and high inflation.
The Dominican Republic enters the CAFTA-DR free trade agreement with the United States and Central America, boosting trade and investment.
A Constitutional Court ruling retroactively strips citizenship from many Dominicans of Haitian descent, sparking an international human rights controversy.
Luis Abinader of the Modern Revolutionary Party wins the presidency, ending 16 years of PLD rule amid the COVID-19 pandemic.
The government begins constructing a border wall along the frontier with Haiti to control migration and smuggling.
President Luis Abinader is re-elected to a second term in a landslide victory.
